GateDocument.TextWithNodes: "Your most pressing skincare questions answered by a skin scientist", "Decode labels like a pro with this smart skin guide.", "For more than 15 years, skin scientist Terri Vinson has been schooling clinicians, medical teams and skin savvy consumers alike, on skin health and formulating products, both for other brands and for those bearing her own label. More than a decade ago she coined and trademarked the phrase "clean science," to describe her science-meets-nature philosophies around potent, effective products, long before it was a buzzword. Vinson combines the infinite curiosity of a scientist with a true passion to educate others, and the result is her new book Skinformation. The paperback guide is an in depth look at how skin health works, which ingredients are worth the hype and a troubleshooting section to answer a litany of common skin concerns like aging, acne, pores and beyond. Basically, if you buy skincare products, you probably need to read this first. Vogue asked Vinson to break down the essential ingredients to smarter skin. What's the most important element of skincare to get right? Skin concerns will vary with our age and environment, but the most common skin concern in Australia is sun damage. The answer, of course, is protection. By far the best sun protection is four walls and a roof, but failing that, I recommend physical sunscreen in the form of zinc oxide at a minimum of 20 per cent dosage to give good broad-spectrum protection. As a cosmetic chemist, I prefer not to use the chemical absorbing sunscreens in my lab as they are more likely to cause irritation and photosensitivity reactions. If you're unprotected, any sunscreen is better than none at all, however, zinc oxide is always my first choice. Which ingredients are worth the hype? One of the most exciting cosmeceutical ingredients available is the new retinoid on the block, hydroxy pinacolone retinoate (HPR). It behaves in a remarkably similar fashion to prescription tretinoin, as it binds directly to the vitamin A receptors inside our cells. This makes it the most effective over the counter form of vitamin A. I've been using it in my lab for over a year and the results have been excellent. And niacinamide is hands-down my number one. I refer to vitamin B3 as the great skin multitasker as it's able to perform so many amazing functions for our skin. It's so important for making our skin barrier strong. It increases the production of natural skin lipids called ceramides, so our skin is better at holding water. It also increases the immunity of our skin to fight invaders, reduces the severity of acne, reduces the appearance of uneven skin tone and increases collagen production for a more youthful and luminous complexion. Seriously, what's not to love? Which products need to be in every effective beauty regimen? Think A,B,C to Zinc! Don't even bother with any other product if you don't have a great broad-spectrum sunscreen, and you know how much I love zinc oxide! In the morning, following cleansing I would ditch traditional toners and use a prebiotic-probiotic pre serum to balance the skin's microbiome. Then protect your skin from those environmental nasties with a great antioxidant. I can't go past vitamin C but try and avoid l-ascorbic acid in a water-based serum as it is highly unstable when in water. Then follow with sunscreen, either built into your daily moisturiser or in your mineral makeup. What was your motivation to write Skinformation? Skinformation demystifies ingredients and product labels, and cuts through hype and myths. It teaches the science of skin in a no-nonsense style and gives an honest overview of the truth behind skincare, so readers can cut-out the products and treatments that don't serve them and focus on what really works. There are lots of 'good skin' recipes in the book. Which is your personal favourite? My great skin salad dressing. It is super easy to make and you can add your own twist for added flavour. Good oils are essential for great skin, both on the skin and in our food, and the secret to this dressing is using a good quality flax seed oil, as it offers the perfect balance of omega 3, 6 and 9 fatty acids. Which is your favourite chapter? When I wrote chapter nine What's really in your bathroom cabinet I honestly high-fived myself. I've never been able to find concise information about exactly how to decipher those confusing product labels, packaging symbols and complex ingredient lists. So, I did my best to decode what's in your bathroom cabinet. I've also added some important tips that will make a significant impact on how you shop for and choose your skincare."
